name: The Suntouch Veranda
image_prompt: An open-air luxury veranda cantilevered off the stone face of a cliff-side mansion four kilometers above a cloud layer, late afternoon golden hour. White marble floor flaring warm gold on its sun-facing edge. A canopy of dark resinous wooden beams overhead. Polished brass railings along the edge, beyond which a slow-moving sea of pearl and rose clouds extends to the horizon, occasionally threaded by the running lights of a distant flying aircar. Worn leather lounge chairs and low glass tables. A whiskey decanter and two crystal glasses on a table. A potted jasmine. A thin tracery of recessed light under the canopy at candlelight warmth. Empty, no people. Colors: white marble, polished brass gold, deep resinous wood, pearl-and-rose cloud sea, amber sunset. Opulent Altered-Carbon-style atmosphere.
location: An open-air terrace cantilevered off the western face of a Meth estate, four kilometers above the surface of what used to be San Francisco. The veranda hangs in the clean air just below the cloud layer, anchored to the stone face of the host's home by polished steel pylons and an arrogance about engineering. The estate has been in the host family since before the second sleeve law was repealed. The veranda has been added, removed, and re-added across nine generations.
time: Late afternoon, slowly. The sun is the warm color of an old gold coin, dropping toward the haze. Down at street level somewhere far below, it is already night.
weather: Up here the air is dry, thin, exquisitely clean, a few degrees colder than anyone is dressed for. A constant mild wind slides off the cliff face. Beneath the veranda the cloud floor is a slow-moving sea of pearl gray and rose, occasionally torn open by the running lights of an aircar threading the lanes below.

atmosphere.lighting: The horizontal afternoon light is doing the work that lighting designers cannot. The terrace's white marble flares warm gold along its western edge and falls into deep cool blue at its eastern. As the sun drops, recessed lamps in the railing wake themselves up one by one. A thin tracery of fiber along the underside of the canopy holds steady at a precise candlelight tone the host's grandfather selected once and never changed.
atmosphere.sound: The wind, mostly. Far below, the muffled drone of distant traffic, more felt than heard. A quartet of strings playing somewhere inside the house, not loud enough to identify the piece. Glass on glass. The occasional hiss of a fountain whose source is hidden in the shrubbery. None of the voices that should be in the air at this altitude are.
atmosphere.crowd: The veranda was sized for entertaining the kind of company the host keeps. It accommodates whoever has been invited up the cliff without comment. The space leaves room around every shoulder.
atmosphere.smell: Old leather from the chairs. The mineral smell of high-altitude air. A whisper of jasmine from the gardens further along the cliff. Cigar tobacco that has been imported by name for two centuries. Beneath everything, something warm and resinous from the wood of the canopy beams that the host had grown specifically for this terrace and that took ninety years to mature.

layout.main_loggia: A long marble platform under a cedar canopy, framed by knee-high planters of pale grasses that sway against the railing. A single low table the length of the platform, polished to mirror, ringed by deep leather chairs the color of dark honey. Whoever sits here can see clear to the curve of the world. A decanter of something amber sits at the center because a decanter has always sat at the center.
layout.western_railing: A polished brass rail running the length of the cliff edge with no glass between it and the four-kilometer drop. Standing at it is an act of trust in metallurgy. The rail is warm to the touch from the afternoon sun. Looking down at the cloud floor, the eye can pick out the faint geometric grid of the city below when the haze parts.
layout.private_pavilion: A small enclosed alcove at the southern end of the veranda, walled in carved wood and translucent screens, just large enough for two armchairs facing each other across a low brass tray. The air inside is warmer and smells more strongly of cedar. Conversations held here do not carry to the loggia. The host built this room for negotiations he did not want the staff to overhear.
layout.telescope_emplacement: A heavy brass refractor on a teak tripod near the eastern railing, polished daily, never used. Aimed at a fixed point in the haze that turns out, when the air clears, to be the seat of the city government far below. The host calls it the listening end. He is joking. Probably.
layout.service_alcove: A small recess set into the cliff face behind the loggia, screened by a hanging carpet, where a silent attendant in a charcoal uniform refreshes the decanter and removes empty glasses. The attendant has been with the family across two sleeves and three generations. He never speaks unless addressed and answers in Russian regardless of the question.

objects_of_note: A bound leather guest book on a small marble pedestal at the entrance to the loggia, signed by every visitor for the past hundred and forty years. The earliest entries are in fountain pen. Some of the more recent names appear more than once, separated by decades, in different handwritings.
A single pair of antique field glasses on the table, lenses turned inward as if someone had been examining the decanter rather than the view.
A small silver dish at the head of the table containing six unlabeled black capsules. Nobody takes one. Nobody comments. They have been there for three weeks.
A potted bonsai juniper on the eastern railing that the host's first wife brought with her to the estate two centuries ago. It has been re-potted forty times. It is still the same tree.
A holographic stock ticker projected discreetly above the service alcove, displaying instruments that ceased trading on the surface markets a hundred years ago. The host watches them out of habit.
mood: Decadent and weightless. The altitude erases the urgency of mortal scales. Whoever sits at this table has been given a temporary loan of perspective: from up here, problems flatten into geography, and geography looks managed. The host's wealth is not displayed; it is implied by the impossibility of the terrace existing at all. A conversation here will be patient, cold-blooded, and longer than it intends to be. Decisions made above the cloud floor have a way of feeling clean. They land messily.
